title: Starting a Fire (Friction/Bow Drill)
immediate_steps: Gather dry tinder (bird nests, dry grass, shaved bark)
Create a bow using a flexible branch and a shoelace or cord
Make a fireboard from dry, soft wood with a small notch
Carve a straight, hard wood spindle
Use a bearing block (stone or hard wood) to hold the top of the spindle
Wrap the bowstring around the spindle and move the bow back and forth rapidly to create friction
Once an ember forms, drop it into the tinder bundle and blow gently until it ignites
avoid: Do not use wet or green wood
Do not blow too hard and extinguish the ember
Do not give up quickly (friction fires require stamina)

title: Fire Safety and Extinguishing
immediate_steps: Clear a 10-foot radius around your fire pit of all flammable material
Keep the fire small and manageable
To extinguish, drown the fire with water
Stir the ashes and embers with dirt and more water
Feel the ashes with the back of your hand to ensure it is completely cold before leaving
avoid: Do not leave a fire unattended
Do not build a fire on peaty soil (fire can burn underground)
Do not use river rocks (they can explode when heated)
